concept: A Czech CEO at a wooden desk reviewing turnover cost sheets, calculator and pen nearby, with one empty office chair in the blurred background to symbolize silent attrition.
A team meeting in a modern office where one employee sits slightly withdrawn by the window while the manager notices subtle disengagement cues.
Friendly office atmosphere with smiling colleagues, while one talented employee quietly packs a laptop and notebook, suggesting that satisfaction alone does not prevent departure.
A direct manager leading a small team standup, with body language clearly shaping the room's energy and engagement.
A thick annual survey report gathering dust on a conference table beside a neglected fruit bowl, symbolizing expensive reports that lead nowhere.
HR and team leaders in a Czech office planning a pulse rollout with sticky notes, calendars, and a clean implementation board on the wall.
An employee privately completing an anonymous survey on a phone in a calm workspace, with privacy and trust emphasized through composition and lighting.
A motivated office team naturally joining a quick feedback check-in on their devices, showing momentum and voluntary participation without pressure.
An HR lead and manager mapping a clear 30-day action plan on a wall calendar with cards and colored markers after reviewing survey insights.
An empathetic one-on-one between HR and a manager reviewing printed team insights together, with calm, non-defensive body language.
Close-up editorial desk scene with thoughtfully arranged survey question cards, sticky notes, and a pen, conveying deliberate question design.
An office worker looking mentally drained by repeated notifications and stacked feedback forms, illustrating survey fatigue without exaggeration.
A leader comparing several trend snapshots on a laptop and printed charts, focused on movement over time rather than one big number.
Analysts clustering colored cards and sticky notes into clear groups by team, role, and seniority on a meeting table.
Two data-minded colleagues debating in front of a whiteboard full of arrows, dots, and branching causes, symbolizing careful causal reasoning.
One scene showing the three viewpoints of Echo Pulse: an employee on a phone, a manager on a laptop, and an executive reviewing results nearby.
An executive opening a laptop with fresh incoming results early in the morning, coffee on the table, capturing the immediacy of first-day insight.
A focused leadership review in a compact boardroom, with the team aligned around a short agenda and clear priorities.
An HR specialist reorganizing a messy pulse rollout board into a clean plan, visually moving from common mistakes to clarity.
A single small team in a pilot-style standup session, experimenting with a new process in a low-risk, practical setting.
A clean corporate scene emphasizing access boundaries and privacy, with HR, CEO, and manager separated by glass and layered distance rather than explicit text.
A crisp desk scene with a stopwatch, phone, laptop, and a few scheduled calendar blocks, showing realistic time budgeting in minutes.
A calm HR lead handling a tense meeting room moment after bad results, with composed posture and a clear crisis plan.
A facilitator sorting anonymous comment cards into piles, carefully distinguishing emotional venting from useful signal.
A decision-focused office scene comparing a generic simple form on one screen with richer insight materials on another, without visible brand names or readable UI text.
An employee physically present in an open office but emotionally checked out, detached from the collaborative energy around them.
A high-performing professional working late with visible cognitive overload, empty coffee cups, and fatigue, framed as an operational risk rather than a melodrama.
A new hire during the first weeks, guided by a buddy at a freshly prepared workstation, conveying structured onboarding and early performance support.
Company values posters blurred in the background while real team behavior and collaboration happen authentically in the foreground.
A middle manager visually positioned between executives and team members, carrying tension from both sides while trying to keep work moving.
